problem: Given positive integers $q$, define the sequence $p(q)$ by the following rules: $p(1) = 1$, $p(2q) = p(q)$, and $p(2q+1) = (-1)^q p(q)$. Find the value of \[ \sum_{q=1}^{2173} p(q) p(q+2). \]
solution: Note that  \begin{align*} p(2k+1)p(2k+3) &= (-1)^k p(k) (-1)^{k+1} p(k+1) \\ &= -p(k)p(k+1) \\  &= -p(2k)p(2k+2). \end{align*} It follows that $\sum_{q=2}^{2173} p(q)p(q+2) = \sum_{k=1}^{1086} (p(2k)p(2k+2)+p(2k+1)p(2k+3)) = 0$, and so the desired sum is $p(1)p(3) = \boxed{-1}$.

problem: Given positive integers $j$, define the sequence $f(j)$ by the following rules: $f(1) = 1$, $f(2j) = f(j)$, and $f(2j+1) = (-1)^j f(j)$. Find the value of \[ \sum_{j=1}^{4837} f(j) f(j+2). \]
solution: Note that  \begin{align*} f(2k+1)f(2k+3) &= (-1)^k f(k) (-1)^{k+1} f(k+1) \\ &= -f(k)f(k+1) \\  &= -f(2k)f(2k+2). \end{align*} It follows that $\sum_{j=2}^{4837} f(j)f(j+2) = \sum_{k=1}^{2418} (f(2k)f(2k+2)+f(2k+1)f(2k+3)) = 0$, and so the desired sum is $f(1)f(3) = \boxed{-1}$.

problem: Given positive integers $s$, define the sequence $p(s)$ by the following rules: $p(1) = 1$, $p(2s) = p(s)$, and $p(2s+1) = (-1)^s p(s)$. Find the value of \[ \sum_{s=1}^{3489} p(s) p(s+2). \]
solution: Note that  \begin{align*} p(2k+1)p(2k+3) &= (-1)^k p(k) (-1)^{k+1} p(k+1) \\ &= -p(k)p(k+1) \\  &= -p(2k)p(2k+2). \end{align*} It follows that $\sum_{s=2}^{3489} p(s)p(s+2) = \sum_{k=1}^{1744} (p(2k)p(2k+2)+p(2k+1)p(2k+3)) = 0$, and so the desired sum is $p(1)p(3) = \boxed{-1}$.

problem: Given positive integers $m$, define the sequence $w(m)$ by the following rules: $w(1) = 1$, $w(2m) = w(m)$, and $w(2m+1) = (-1)^m w(m)$. Find the value of \[ \sum_{m=1}^{1847} w(m) w(m+2). \]
solution: Note that  \begin{align*} w(2k+1)w(2k+3) &= (-1)^k w(k) (-1)^{k+1} w(k+1) \\ &= -w(k)w(k+1) \\  &= -w(2k)w(2k+2). \end{align*} It follows that $\sum_{m=2}^{1847} w(m)w(m+2) = \sum_{k=1}^{923} (w(2k)w(2k+2)+w(2k+1)w(2k+3)) = 0$, and so the desired sum is $w(1)w(3) = \boxed{-1}$.

problem: Given positive integers $x$, define the sequence $j(x)$ by the following rules: $j(1) = 1$, $j(2x) = j(x)$, and $j(2x+1) = (-1)^x j(x)$. Find the value of \[ \sum_{x=1}^{2183} j(x) j(x+2). \]
solution: Note that  \begin{align*} j(2k+1)j(2k+3) &= (-1)^k j(k) (-1)^{k+1} j(k+1) \\ &= -j(k)j(k+1) \\  &= -j(2k)j(2k+2). \end{align*} It follows that $\sum_{x=2}^{2183} j(x)j(x+2) = \sum_{k=1}^{1091} (j(2k)j(2k+2)+j(2k+1)j(2k+3)) = 0$, and so the desired sum is $j(1)j(3) = \boxed{-1}$.

problem: Consider an odd integer $U \geq 1$. Alice and Bob play a game by taking turns, starting with Alice. The game board consists of $U$ spaces arranged in a straight line, all of which are initially empty. On each turn, a player can choose one of the following actions: \begin{itemize} \item place a stone in an empty space, or \item remove a stone from a nonempty space $s$, place a stone in the nearest empty space to the left of $s$ (if available), and place a stone in the nearest empty space to the right of $s$ (if available). \end{itemize} A move is only allowed if it results in a position that has not been seen before in the game. The player who cannot make a move loses. Assuming both players play optimally, what moves can Alice make on her first turn? Given that the spaces are numbered from 1 to $U, which space should Alice choose to place or remove a stone?
solution: We start with some terminology.  %Divide the playing area into the \emph{left half}, the \emph{central %space}, and the \emph{right half}. By a \emph{block} of stones, we mean a (possibly empty) sequence of stones occupying consecutive spaces. By the \emph{extremal blocks}, we mean the (possibly empty) maximal blocks adjacent to the left and right ends of the playing area.  We refer to a legal move consisting of placing a stone in an empty space as a move of \emph{type 1}, and any other legal move as being of \emph{type 2}. For $i=0,\dots,U$, let $P_i$ be the collection of positions containing $i$ stones. Define the \emph{end zone} as the union $Z = P_{U-1} \cup P_U$. In this language, we make the following observations. \begin{itemize} \item Any move of type 1 from $P_i$ ends in $P_{i+1}$. \item Any move of type 2 from $P_U$ ends in $P_{U-1}$. \item For $i < U$, any move of type 2 from $P_i$ ends in $P_i \cup P_{i+1}$. \item At this point, we see that  the number of stones cannot decrease until we reach the end zone. \item For $i < U-1$, if we start at a position in $P_i$ where the extremal blocks have length $a,b$, then the only possible moves to $P_i$ decrease one of $a,b$ while leaving the other unchanged (because they are separated by at least two empty spaces). In particular, no repetition is possible within $P_i$, so the number of stones must eventually increase to $i+1$. \item From any position in the end zone, the legal moves are precisely to the other positions in the end zone which have not previously occurred. Consequently, after the first move into the end zone, the rest of the game consists of enumerating all positions in the end zone in some order. \item At this point, we may change the rules without affecting the outcome by eliminating the rule on repetitions and declaring that the first player to move into the end zone loses (because $\# Z = U+1$ is even). \end{itemize}  To determine who wins in each position, number the spaces of the board $1,\dots,U$ from left to right. Define the \emph{weight} of a position to be the sum of the labels of the occupied spaces, reduced modulo $U+1$. For any given position outside of the end zone,  for each $s=1,\dots,U$ there is a unique move that adds $s$ to the weight: if $s$ is empty that a move of type 1 there does the job. Otherwise, $s$ inhabits a block running from $i+1$ to $j-1$ with $i$ and $j$ empty (or equal to $0$ or $U+1$), so the type 2 move at $i+j-s$ (which belongs to the same block) does the job.  We now verify that a position of weight $s$ outside of the end zone is a win for the player to move if and only if $s \neq (U+1)/2$. We check this for positions in $P_i$ for $i = n-2, \dots, 0$ by descending induction. For positions in $P_{U-2}$, the only safe moves are in the extremal blocks; we may thus analyze these positions as two-pile Nim with pile sizes equal to the lengths of the extremal blocks. In particular, a position is a win for the player to move if and only if the extremal blocks are unequal, in which case the winning move is to equalize the blocks. In other words, a position is a win for the player to move unless the empty spaces are at $s$ and $U+1-s$ for some $s \in \{1,\dots,(U-1)/2\}$, and indeed these are precisely the positions for which the weight equals $(1 + \cdots + U) - (U+1) \equiv (U+1)/2 \pmod{U+1}$. Given the analysis of positions in $P_{i+1}$ for some $i$, it is clear that if a position in $P_i$ has weight $s \neq (U+1)/2$, there is a winning move of weight $t$ where $s+t \equiv (U+1)/2 \pmod{U}$, whereas if $s = (U+1)/2$ then no move leads to a winning position.  It thus follows that the unique winning move for Alice at her first turn is to move at the central space, as claimed. We show that the only winning first move for Alice is to place a stone in the central space, i.e., the \boxed{(n+1)/2} space.

problem: Consider the $i \times i$ matrix $K$ where the element in the $t$-th row and $k$-th column is defined as \[ \frac{1}{\min(t,k)}. \] Given $1 \leq t,k \leq i$, determine $\det(K)$.
solution: Let $v_1,\ldots,v_i$ denote the rows of $K$. The determinant is  unchanged if we replace $v_i$ by $v_i-v_{i-1}$, and then $v_{i-1}$ by  $v_{i-1}-v_{i-2}$, and so forth, eventually replacing $v_k$ by  $v_k-v_{k-1}$ for $k\geq 2$. Since $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ agree in their  first $k-1$ entries, and the $k$-th entry of $v_k-v_{k-1}$ is  $\frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}$, the result of these row operations is an upper triangular  matrix with diagonal entries $1,\frac{1}{2}-1,\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{1}t-\frac{1}{i-1}$. The  determinant is then \begin{align*} \prod_{k=2}^i \left( \frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}\right) &= \prod_{k=2}^i \left(\frac{-1}{k(k-1)} \right) \\ &=  \boxed{\frac{(-1)^{i-1}}{(i-1)!i!}}. \end{align*}

problem: Consider the $a \times a$ matrix $T$ where the element in the $h$-th row and $p$-th column is defined as \[ \frac{1}{\min(h,p)}. \] Given $1 \leq h,p \leq a$, determine $\det(T)$.
solution: Let $v_1,\ldots,v_a$ denote the rows of $T$. The determinant is  unchanged if we replace $v_a$ by $v_a-v_{a-1}$, and then $v_{a-1}$ by  $v_{a-1}-v_{a-2}$, and so forth, eventually replacing $v_k$ by  $v_k-v_{k-1}$ for $k\geq 2$. Since $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ agree in their  first $k-1$ entries, and the $k$-th entry of $v_k-v_{k-1}$ is  $\frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}$, the result of these row operations is an upper triangular  matrix with diagonal entries $1,\frac{1}{2}-1,\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{1}{a}-\frac{1}{a-1}$. The  determinant is then \begin{align*} \prod_{k=2}^a \left( \frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}\right) &= \prod_{k=2}^a \left(\frac{-1}{k(k-1)} \right) \\ &=  \boxed{\frac{(-1)^{a-1}}{(a-1)!a!}}. \end{align*}

problem: Consider the $l \times l$ matrix $U$ where the element in the $r$-th row and $d$-th column is defined as \[ \frac{1}{\min(r,d)}. \] Given $1 \leq r,d \leq l$, determine $\det(U)$.
solution: Let $v_1,\ldots,v_l$ denote the rows of $U$. The determinant is  unchanged if we replace $v_l$ by $v_l-v_{l-1}$, and then $v_{l-1}$ by  $v_{l-1}-v_{l-2}$, and so forth, eventually replacing $v_k$ by  $v_k-v_{k-1}$ for $k\geq 2$. Since $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ agree in their  first $k-1$ entries, and the $k$-th entry of $v_k-v_{k-1}$ is  $\frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}$, the result of these row operations is an upper triangular  matrix with diagonal entries $1,\frac{1}{2}-1,\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{1}{l}-\frac{1}{l-1}$. The  determinant is then \begin{align*} \prod_{k=2}^l \left( \frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}\right) &= \prod_{k=2}^l \left(\frac{-1}{k(k-1)} \right) \\ &=  \boxed{\frac{(-1)^{l-1}}{(l-1)!l!}}. \end{align*}

problem: Consider the $i \times i$ matrix $C$ where the element in the $y$-th row and $w$-th column is defined as \[ \frac{1}{\min(y,w)}. \] Given $1 \leq y,w \leq i$, determine $\det(C)$.
solution: Let $v_1,\ldots,v_i$ denote the rows of $C$. The determinant is  unchanged if we replace $v_i$ by $v_i-v_{i-1}$, and then $v_{i-1}$ by  $v_{i-1}-v_{i-2}$, and so forth, eventually replacing $v_k$ by  $v_k-v_{k-1}$ for $k\geq 2$. Since $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ agree in their  first $k-1$ entries, and the $k$-th entry of $v_k-v_{k-1}$ is  $\frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}$, the result of these row operations is an upper triangular  matrix with diagonal entries $1,\frac{1}{2}-1,\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{1}y-\frac{1}{i-1}$. The  determinant is then \begin{align*} \prod_{k=2}^i \left( \frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}\right) &= \prod_{k=2}^i \left(\frac{-1}{k(k-1)} \right) \\ &=  \boxed{\frac{(-1)^{i-1}}{(i-1)!i!}}. \end{align*}

problem: Consider the $p \times p$ matrix $N$ where the element in the $m$-th row and $k$-th column is defined as \[ \frac{1}{\min(m,k)}. \] Given $1 \leq m,k \leq p$, determine $\det(N)$.
solution: Let $v_1,\ldots,v_p$ denote the rows of $N$. The determinant is  unchanged if we replace $v_p$ by $v_p-v_{p-1}$, and then $v_{p-1}$ by  $v_{p-1}-v_{p-2}$, and so forth, eventually replacing $v_k$ by  $v_k-v_{k-1}$ for $k\geq 2$. Since $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ agree in their  first $k-1$ entries, and the $k$-th entry of $v_k-v_{k-1}$ is  $\frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}$, the result of these row operations is an upper triangular  matrix with diagonal entries $1,\frac{1}{2}-1,\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{1}{p}-\frac{1}{p-1}$. The  determinant is then \begin{align*} \prod_{k=2}^p \left( \frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}\right) &= \prod_{k=2}^p \left(\frac{-1}{k(k-1)} \right) \\ &=  \boxed{\frac{(-1)^{p-1}}{(p-1)!p!}}. \end{align*}

problem: Consider a random variable $B$ that only takes nonnegative integer values. It is given that $E\left[ B \right] = 1$, $E\left[ B^2 \right] = 2$, and $E \left[ B^3 \right] = 5$.(Here $E\left[ Y \right]$ denotes the expectation of the random variable $Y$.) Minimize the probbality of of $B=0$. Under this condition what is the probability that $B = 75529$?
solution: Let $a_n = P(B=n)$; we want the minimum value for $a_75529$. If we write  $S_k = \sum_{n=1}^\infty n^k a_n$, then the given expectation values  imply that $S_1 = 1$, $S_2 = 2$, $S_3 = 5$. Now define $f(n) = 11n-6n^2+n^3$, and note that $f(0) = 0$, $f(1)=f(2)=f(3)=6$, and  $f(n)>6$ for $n\geq 4$; thus $4 = 11S_1-6S_2+S_3 = \sum_{n=1}^\infty f(n)a_n \geq 6 \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$. Since $\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n = 1$, it  follows that $a_0 \geq \frac{1}{3}$. Equality is achieved  when $a_0=\frac{1}{3}$, $a_1=\frac{1}{2}$, $a_3=\frac{1}{6}$, and $a_n = 0$ for all other $n$,  and so the answer is $\boxed{0}$.

problem: Consider a function $j$ defined on the interval $[1,11960]$ such that $-1 \leq j(o) \leq 1$ for every $o$ and $\int_1^11960 j(o)\,do = 0$. Determine the maximum value of $\int_1^11960 \frac{j(o)}{o}\,do$.  \,
solution: In all solutions, we assume that the function $j$ is integrable. Let $g(o)$ be $1$ for $1\leq o\leq 5980$ and $-1$ for $5980<o\leq 11960$, and  define $h(o)=g(o)-j(o)$. Then $\int_1^11960 h(o)\,do = 0$ and $h(o) \geq 0$  for $1\leq o\leq 5980$, $h(o) \leq 0$ for $5980<o\leq 11960$. Now \begin{align*} \int_1^11960 \frac{h(o)}{o}\,dx & = \int_1^5980 \frac{|h(o)|}{o}\,do - \int_5980^11960  \frac{|h(o)|}{o}\,do\\ &\geq \int_1^5980 \frac{|h(o)|}{5980}\,do - \int_5980^11960 \frac{|h(o)|}{5980}\,do = 0, \end{align*} and thus $\int_1^11960 \frac{j(o)}{o}\,do \leq \int_1^11960 \frac{g(o)}{o}\,do =  2\log 5980-\log 11960 = \log \frac{35760400}{11960}$. Since $g(o)$ achieves the upper bound, the answer is $\boxed{\log \frac{35760400}{11960}}$.

problem: Consider a function $o$ defined on the interval $[1,53524]$ such that $-1 \leq o(v) \leq 1$ for every $v$ and $\int_1^53524 o(v)\,dv = 0$. Determine the maximum value of $\int_1^53524 \frac{o(v)}{v}\,dv$.  \,
solution: In all solutions, we assume that the function $o$ is integrable. Let $g(v)$ be $1$ for $1\leq v\leq 26762$ and $-1$ for $26762<v\leq 53524$, and  define $h(v)=g(v)-o(v)$. Then $\int_1^53524 h(v)\,dv = 0$ and $h(v) \geq 0$  for $1\leq v\leq 26762$, $h(v) \leq 0$ for $26762<v\leq 53524$. Now \begin{align*} \int_1^53524 \frac{h(v)}{v}\,dx & = \int_1^26762 \frac{|h(v)|}{v}\,dv - \int_26762^53524  \frac{|h(v)|}{v}\,dv\\ &\geq \int_1^26762 \frac{|h(v)|}{26762}\,dv - \int_26762^53524 \frac{|h(v)|}{26762}\,dv = 0, \end{align*} and thus $\int_1^53524 \frac{o(v)}{v}\,dv \leq \int_1^53524 \frac{g(v)}{v}\,dv =  2\log 26762-\log 53524 = \log \frac{716204644}{53524}$. Since $g(v)$ achieves the upper bound, the answer is $\boxed{\log \frac{716204644}{53524}}$.

problem: In the 75th annual Putnam Games, Patniss and Keeta are engaged in a mathematical competition. They alternately select an element from the set of invertible $r \times r$ matrices with entries in the field $\mathbb{Z}/p \mathbb{Z}$, where $r$ is a given positive integer and $p$ is a given prime number.The game follows these rules: \begin{enumerate} \item[(1)] A player cannot select an element that has already been chosen by either player in previous turns. \item[(2)] A player can only select an element that commutes with all previously chosen elements. \item[(3)]A player who cannot select an element on their turn loses the game. \end{enumerate} Keeta goes first. Determine the smallest value of $p$ for which Keeta wins, the smallest value of $p$ for which Patness wins, and compute the product of these two values (Your answer may depend on $r$ and $p$.)
solution: We first analyze the analogous game played using an arbitrary finite group $G$. Recall that for any subset $S$ of $G$, the set of elements $g \in G$ which commute with all elements of $S$ forms a subgroup $Z(S)$ of $G$, called the \emph{centralizer} (or \emph{commutant}) of $S$.  At any given point in the game, the set $S$ of previously chosen elements is contained in $Z(S)$.Initially $S = \emptyset$ and $Z(S) = G$; after each turn, $S$ is increased by one element and $Z(S)$ is replaced by a subgroup. In particular, if the order of $Z(S)$ is odd at some point, it remains odd thereafter; conversely, if $S$ contains an element of even order, then the order of $Z(S)$ remains even thereafter. Therefore, any element $g \in G$ for which $Z(\{g\})$ hasodd order is a winning first move for Keeta, while any other first move by Keeta loses if Patness responds with some $h \in Z(\{g\})$ of even order (e.g., an element of a 2-Sylow subgroup of $Z(\{g\})$). In both cases, the win is guaranteed no matter what moves follow.  Now let $G$ be the group of invertible $r \times r$ matrices with entries in $\ZZ/p\ZZ$.If $p>2$, then $Z(S)$ will always contain the scalar matrix $-1$ of order 2, so the win for Patness is guaranteed. (An explicit winning strategy is to answer any move $g$ with the move $-g$.)  If $p=2$, we establish the existence of $g \in G$ such that $Z(\{g\})$ has odd order using the existence of an irreducible polynomial $P(x)$ of degree $r$ over $\ZZ/p\ZZ$.We construct an $r \times r$ matrix over $\ZZ/p\ZZ$ with characteristic polynomial $P(x)$ by taking the \emph{companion matrix} of $P(x)$: write $P(x) = x^r + P_{r-1} x^{r-1} + \cdots + P_0$ and set \[ g = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & -P_0 \\ 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & -P_1 \\ 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & -P_2 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & -P_{r-1} \end{pmatrix}. \]In particular, $\det(g) = (-1)^r P_0 \neq 0$, so $g \in G$. Over an algebraic closure of $\ZZ/p \ZZ$, $g$ becomes diagonalizable with distinct eigenvalues, so any matrix commuting with $g$ must also be diagonalizable, and hence of odd order. In particular, $Z(\{g\})$ is of odd order, so Keeta has a winning strategy. We show that Keeta wins if $p=2$ and Patness wins if $p>2$ (for all $r$)with the product of the minimum values of $p$ being \boxed{6}.

We show that Patniss wins if $p=2$ and Keeta wins if $p>2$ (for all $d$)with the product of the minimum values of $p$ being \boxed{6}.

problem: In the 75th annual Putnam Games, Patniss and Keeta are engaged in a mathematical competition. They alternately select an element from the set of invertible $d \times d$ matrices with entries in the field $\mathbb{Z}/p \mathbb{Z}$, where $d$ is a given positive integer and $p$ is a given prime number.The game follows these rules: \begin{enumerate} \item[(1)] A player cannot select an element that has already been chosen by either player in previous turns. \item[(2)] A player can only select an element that commutes with all previously chosen elements. \item[(3)]A player who cannot select an element on their turn loses the game. \end{enumerate} Keeta goes first. Determine the smallest value of $p$ for which Keeta wins, the smallest value of $p$ for which Patness wins, and compute the product of these two values (Your answer may depend on $d$ and $p$.)
solution: We first analyze the analogous game played using an arbitrary finite group $G$. Recall that for any subset $S$ of $G$, the set of elements $g \in G$ which commute with all elements of $S$ forms a subgroup $Z(S)$ of $G$, called the \emph{centralizer} (or \emph{commutant}) of $S$.  At any given point in the game, the set $S$ of previously chosen elements is contained in $Z(S)$.Initially $S = \emptyset$ and $Z(S) = G$; after each turn, $S$ is increased by one element and $Z(S)$ is replaced by a subgroup. In particular, if the order of $Z(S)$ is odd at some point, it remains odd thereafter; conversely, if $S$ contains an element of even order, then the order of $Z(S)$ remains even thereafter. Therefore, any element $g \in G$ for which $Z(\{g\})$ hasodd order is a winning first move for Keeta, while any other first move by Keeta loses if Patness responds with some $h \in Z(\{g\})$ of even order (e.g., an element of a 2-Sylow subgroup of $Z(\{g\})$). In both cases, the win is guaranteed no matter what moves follow.  Now let $G$ be the group of invertible $d \times d$ matrices with entries in $\ZZ/p\ZZ$.If $p>2$, then $Z(S)$ will always contain the scalar matrix $-1$ of order 2, so the win for Patness is guaranteed. (An explicit winning strategy is to answer any move $g$ with the move $-g$.)  If $p=2$, we establish the existence of $g \in G$ such that $Z(\{g\})$ has odd order using the existence of an irreducible polynomial $P(x)$ of degree $d$ over $\ZZ/p\ZZ$.We construct an $d \times d$ matrix over $\ZZ/p\ZZ$ with characteristic polynomial $P(x)$ by taking the \emph{companion matrix} of $P(x)$: write $P(x) = x^d + P_{d-1} x^{d-1} + \cdots + P_0$ and set \[ g = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & -P_0 \\ 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & -P_1 \\ 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & -P_2 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & -P_{d-1} \end{pmatrix}. \]In particular, $\det(g) = (-1)^d P_0 \neq 0$, so $g \in G$. Over an algebraic closure of $\ZZ/p \ZZ$, $g$ becomes diagonalizable with distinct eigenvalues, so any matrix commuting with $g$ must also be diagonalizable, and hence of odd order. In particular, $Z(\{g\})$ is of odd order, so Keeta has a winning strategy. We show that Keeta wins if $p=2$ and Patness wins if $p>2$ (for all $d$)with the product of the minimum values of $p$ being \boxed{6}.

problem: Consider the sequence defined by $p_0=1$, $p_1=2$, and $p_t=4p_{t-1}-p_{t-2}$ for $t\geq 2$. Determine the smallest odd prime factor of $p_{295}$.
solution: By induction, we have $p_t = ((2+\sqrt{3})^t+(2-\sqrt{3})^t)/2 = (\alpha^t+\beta^t)/2$ for all $t$, where $\alpha = 2+\sqrt{3}$ and $\beta = 2-\sqrt{3}$. Now note that if $k$ is an odd positive integer and $p_t \neq 0$, then $\frac{p_{kt}}{p_t} = \frac{\alpha^{kt}+\beta^{kt}}{\alpha^t+\beta^t} = \alpha^{(k-1)t}-\alpha^{(k-2)t}\beta^t+\cdots-\alpha^t\beta^{(k-2)t}+\beta^{(k-1)t}$. This expression is both rational (because $p_t$ and $p_{kt}$ are integers) and of the form $a+b\sqrt{3}$ for some integers $a,b$ by the expressions for $\alpha,\beta$; it follows that it must be an integer, and so $p_{kt}$ is divisible by $p_t$. Applying this to $t=5$ and $k=403$, we find that $p_{295}$ is divisible by $p_5 = 362$ and thus by $181$. The answer is $\boxed{181}$.

Applying this to $b=5$ and $k=403$, we find that $l_{120}$ is divisible by $l_5 = 362$ and thus by $181$. The answer is $\boxed{181}$.

problem: Determine the smallest positive integer $t$ such that for any polynomial $x(x)$ with integer coefficients and any integer $k$, the value \[ x^{(t)}(k) = \left. \frac{d^t}{dx^t} x(x) \right|_{x=k}  \]  (the $t$-th derivative of $x(x)$ evaluated at $k$) is divisible by 2016.
solution: First suppose that $t$ satisfies the given condition. For $x(x) = x^t$, we have $x^{(t)}(x) = t!$ and thus $t!$ is divisible by $2016$. Since $2016$ is divisible by $2^5$ and $7!$ is not, it follows that $t \geq 8$. Conversely, we claim that $t=8$ works. Indeed, let $x(x) = \sum_{m=0}^n a_m x^m$ be a polynomial with integer coefficients; then if $k$ is any integer,  \begin{align*} x^{(8)}(k) &= \sum_{m=8}^n m(m-1)\cdots (m-7) a_m k^{m-8} \\ &= \sum_{m=8}^n {m\choose 8} 8! a_m k^{m-8} \end{align*} is divisible by $8! = 20 \cdot 2016$, and so $x^{(8)}(k)$ is divisible by $2016$. The answer is $t=\boxed{8}$.

problem: For a given positive integer $m$, define $P(m)$ as the maximum integer $e$ that satisfies the inequality \[ \binom{e}{m-1} > \binom{e-1}{m}. \] Find the value of  \[ \lim_{m \to \infty} \frac{P(m)}{m}. \]
solution: Note that for $e > m+1$, both binomial coefficients are nonzero and their ratio is \begin{align*} {e\choose m-1}/{e-1\choose m} &= \frac{e!m!(e-m-1)!}{(e-1)!(m-1)!(e-m+1)!} \\ &= \frac{em}{(e-m+1)(e-m)}. \end{align*} Thus the condition ${e\choose{m-1}} > {{e-1}\choose m}$ is equivalent to $(e-m+1)(e-m)-em < 0$. The left hand side of this last inequality is a quadratic function of $e$ with roots \begin{align*} \alpha(m) &= \frac{3m-1+\sqrt{5m^2-2m+1}}{2}, \\ \beta(m) &= \frac{3m-1-\sqrt{5m^2-2m+1}}{2}, \end{align*} both of which are real since $5m^2-2m+1 = 4m^2+(m-1)^2 > 0$; it follows that $e$ satisfies the given inequality if and only if $\beta(m) < e < \alpha(m)$. (Note in particular that since $\alpha(m)-\beta(m) = \sqrt{5m^2-2m+1} > 1$, there is always some integer $e$ between $\beta(m)$ and $\alpha(m)$.)   We conclude that $P(m)$ is the greatest integer strictly less than $\alpha(m)$, and thus that  $\alpha(m)-1 \leq P(m) < \alpha(m)$. Now \[ \lim_{m\to\infty} \frac{\alpha(m)}{m} = \lim_{m\to\infty} \frac{3-\frac{1}{m}+\sqrt{5-\frac{2}{m}+\frac{1}{m^2}}}{2} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2} \] and similarly $\lim_{m\to\infty} \frac{\alpha(m)-1}{m} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$, and so by the sandwich theorem, $\lim_{m\to\infty} \frac{P(m)}{m} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$. The answer is $\boxed{\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}}$.

problem: For a given positive integer $l$, define $G(l)$ as the maximum integer $t$ that satisfies the inequality \[ \binom{t}{l-1} > \binom{t-1}{l}. \] Find the value of  \[ \lim_{l \to \infty} \frac{G(l)}{l}. \]
solution: Note that for $t > l+1$, both binomial coefficients are nonzero and their ratio is \begin{align*} {t\choose l-1}/{t-1\choose l} &= \frac{t!l!(t-l-1)!}{(t-1)!(l-1)!(t-l+1)!} \\ &= \frac{tl}{(t-l+1)(t-l)}. \end{align*} Thus the condition ${t\choose{l-1}} > {{t-1}\choose l}$ is equivalent to $(t-l+1)(t-l)-tl < 0$. The left hand side of this last inequality is a quadratic function of $t$ with roots \begin{align*} \alpha(l) &= \frac{3l-1+\sqrt{5l^2-2l+1}}{2}, \\ \beta(l) &= \frac{3l-1-\sqrt{5l^2-2l+1}}{2}, \end{align*} both of which are real since $5l^2-2l+1 = 4l^2+(l-1)^2 > 0$; it follows that $t$ satisfies the given inequality if and only if $\beta(l) < t < \alpha(l)$. (Note in particular that since $\alpha(l)-\beta(l) = \sqrt{5l^2-2l+1} > 1$, there is always some integer $t$ between $\beta(l)$ and $\alpha(l)$.)   We conclude that $G(l)$ is the greatest integer strictly less than $\alpha(l)$, and thus that  $\alpha(l)-1 \leq G(l) < \alpha(l)$. Now \[ \lim_{l\to\infty} \frac{\alpha(l)}{l} = \lim_{l\to\infty} \frac{3-\frac{1}{l}+\sqrt{5-\frac{2}{l}+\frac{1}{l^2}}}{2} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2} \] and similarly $\lim_{l\to\infty} \frac{\alpha(l)-1}{l} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$, and so by the sandwich theorem, $\lim_{l\to\infty} \frac{G(l)}{l} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$. The answer is $\boxed{\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}}$.

problem: For a given positive integer $w$, define $F(w)$ as the maximum integer $q$ that satisfies the inequality \[ \binom{q}{w-1} > \binom{q-1}{w}. \] Find the value of  \[ \lim_{w \to \infty} \frac{F(w)}{w}. \]
solution: Note that for $q > w+1$, both binomial coefficients are nonzero and their ratio is \begin{align*} {q\choose w-1}/{q-1\choose w} &= \frac{q!w!(q-w-1)!}{(q-1)!(w-1)!(q-w+1)!} \\ &= \frac{qw}{(q-w+1)(q-w)}. \end{align*} Thus the condition ${q\choose{w-1}} > {{q-1}\choose w}$ is equivalent to $(q-w+1)(q-w)-qw < 0$. The left hand side of this last inequality is a quadratic function of $q$ with roots \begin{align*} \alpha(w) &= \frac{3w-1+\sqrt{5w^2-2w+1}}{2}, \\ \beta(w) &= \frac{3w-1-\sqrt{5w^2-2w+1}}{2}, \end{align*} both of which are real since $5w^2-2w+1 = 4w^2+(w-1)^2 > 0$; it follows that $q$ satisfies the given inequality if and only if $\beta(w) < q < \alpha(w)$. (Note in particular that since $\alpha(w)-\beta(w) = \sqrt{5w^2-2w+1} > 1$, there is always some integer $q$ between $\beta(w)$ and $\alpha(w)$.)   We conclude that $F(w)$ is the greatest integer strictly less than $\alpha(w)$, and thus that  $\alpha(w)-1 \leq F(w) < \alpha(w)$. Now \[ \lim_{w\to\infty} \frac{\alpha(w)}{w} = \lim_{w\to\infty} \frac{3-\frac{1}{w}+\sqrt{5-\frac{2}{w}+\frac{1}{w^2}}}{2} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2} \] and similarly $\lim_{w\to\infty} \frac{\alpha(w)-1}{w} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$, and so by the sandwich theorem, $\lim_{w\to\infty} \frac{F(w)}{w} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$. The answer is $\boxed{\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}}$.

The left hand side of this last inequality is a quadratic function of $w$ with roots \begin{align*} \alpha(p) &= \frac{3p-1+\sqrt{5p^2-2p+1}}{2}, \\ \beta(p) &= \frac{3p-1-\sqrt{5p^2-2p+1}}{2}, \end{align*} both of which are real since $5p^2-2p+1 = 4p^2+(p-1)^2 > 0$; it follows that $w$ satisfies the given inequality if and only if $\beta(p) < w < \alpha(p)$. We conclude that $M(p)$ is the greatest integer strictly less than $\alpha(p)$, and thus that  $\alpha(p)-1 \leq M(p) < \alpha(p)$. Now \[ \lim_{p\to\infty} \frac{\alpha(p)}{p} = \lim_{p\to\infty} \frac{3-\frac{1}{p}+\sqrt{5-\frac{2}{p}+\frac{1}{p^2}}}{2} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2} \] and similarly $\lim_{p\to\infty} \frac{\alpha(p)-1}{p} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$, and so by the sandwich theorem, $\lim_{p\to\infty} \frac{M(p)}{p} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$. The answer is $\boxed{\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}}$.

problem: For a given positive integer $c$, define $J(c)$ as the maximum integer $o$ that satisfies the inequality \[ \binom{o}{c-1} > \binom{o-1}{c}. \] Find the value of  \[ \lim_{c \to \infty} \frac{J(c)}{c}. \]
solution: Note that for $o > c+1$, both binomial coefficients are nonzero and their ratio is \begin{align*} {o\choose c-1}/{o-1\choose c} &= \frac{o!c!(o-c-1)!}{(o-1)!(c-1)!(o-c+1)!} \\ &= \frac{oc}{(o-c+1)(o-c)}. \end{align*} Thus the condition ${o\choose{c-1}} > {{o-1}\choose c}$ is equivalent to $(o-c+1)(o-c)-oc < 0$. The left hand side of this last inequality is a quadratic function of $o$ with roots \begin{align*} \alpha(c) &= \frac{3c-1+\sqrt{5c^2-2c+1}}{2}, \\ \beta(c) &= \frac{3c-1-\sqrt{5c^2-2c+1}}{2}, \end{align*} both of which are real since $5c^2-2c+1 = 4c^2+(c-1)^2 > 0$; it follows that $o$ satisfies the given inequality if and only if $\beta(c) < o < \alpha(c)$. (Note in particular that since $\alpha(c)-\beta(c) = \sqrt{5c^2-2c+1} > 1$, there is always some integer $o$ between $\beta(c)$ and $\alpha(c)$.)   We conclude that $J(c)$ is the greatest integer strictly less than $\alpha(c)$, and thus that  $\alpha(c)-1 \leq J(c) < \alpha(c)$. Now \[ \lim_{c\to\infty} \frac{\alpha(c)}{c} = \lim_{c\to\infty} \frac{3-\frac{1}{c}+\sqrt{5-\frac{2}{c}+\frac{1}{c^2}}}{2} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2} \] and similarly $\lim_{c\to\infty} \frac{\alpha(c)-1}{c} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$, and so by the sandwich theorem, $\lim_{c\to\infty} \frac{J(c)}{c} = \frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}$. The answer is $\boxed{\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}}$.

problem: Define $Y$ as the smallest set of positive integers satisfying the following conditions: \begin{enumerate} \item[(a)] $2$ is an element of $Y$, \item[(b)] if $i^2$ is in $Y$, then $i$ is also in $Y$, and \item[(c)] if $i$ is in $Y$, then $(i+5)^2$ is also in $Y$. \end{enumerate} Determine the sum of the first $k$ positive integers, in ascending order, that are not elements of $Y$. (Note: $Y$ is the smallest set in the sense that it is a subset of any other set satisfying these conditions.)
solution: If $Y$ consists of all other natural numbers, then $Y$ satisfies the given conditions: note that the only perfect squares not in $Y$ are $1$ and numbers of the form $(5k)^2$ for some positive integer $k$, and it readily follows that both (b) and (c) hold. Now suppose that $T$ is another set of positive integers satisfying (a), (b), and (c). Note from (b) and (c) that if $i \in T$ then $i+5 \in T$, and so $T$ satisfies the following property: \begin{itemize} \item[(d)] if $i\in T$, then $i+5k \in T$ for all $k \geq 0$. \end{itemize}  The following must then be in $T$, with implications labeled by conditions (b) through (d): \begin{gather*} 2 \stackrel{c}{\Rightarrow} 49 \stackrel{c}{\Rightarrow} 54^2 \stackrel{d}{\Rightarrow} 56^2 \stackrel{b}{\Rightarrow} 56 \stackrel{d}{\Rightarrow} 121 \stackrel{b}{\Rightarrow} 11 \ 11 \stackrel{d}{\Rightarrow} 16 \stackrel{b}{\Rightarrow} 4 \stackrel{d}{\Rightarrow} 9 \stackrel{b}{\Rightarrow} 3 \ 16 \stackrel{d}{\Rightarrow} 36 \stackrel{b}{\Rightarrow} 6 \end{gather*} Since $2,3,4,6 \in T$, by (d) $Y \subseteq T$, and so $Y$ is smallest. We claim that the positive integers not in $Y$ are $1$ and all multiples of $5$, making the sum $\boxed{\frac{5k^2-5k+2}{2}}$.

problem: Assume a positive integer $L$ can be represented as the sum of $x$ consecutive positive integers: \[ L = h + (h+1) + (h+2) + \cdots + (h+x-1) \] for $x=2017$ and for no other $x>1$. Among all such positive integers $L$, determine the smallest positive integer $h$ that appears in any of these representations.
solution: Note that the expression for $L$ can be rewritten as $x(2h+x-1)/2$, so that $2L = k(2h+x-1)$. In this expression, $x>1$ by requirement; $x < 2h+x-1$ because $h>1$; and obviously $x$ and $2h+x-1$ have opposite parity. Conversely, for any factorization $2L = mn$ with $1<m<n$ and $m,n$ of opposite parity, we obtain an expression of $L$ in the desired form by taking $x = m$, $h = (n+1-m)/2$.  We now note that $2017$ is prime. (On the exam, solvers would have had to verify this by hand. Since $2017 < 45^2$, this can be done by trial division by the primes up to 43.) For $2L = 2017(2h+2016)$ not to have another expression of the specified form, it must be the case that $2h+2016$ has no odd divisor greater than 1; that is, $2h+2016$ must be a power of 2. This first occurs for $2h+2016=2048$, yielding the claimed result. We prove that the smallest value of $a$ is \boxed{16}.

problem: Identify all pairs of positive integers $(o,x)$ that satisfy the equation \[ \frac{1}{o} + \frac{1}{x} = \frac{3}{2018}. \] Once you have found all such pairs, calculate the ratio obtained by dividing the sum of all o values by the sum of all x values.
solution: By clearing denominators and regrouping, we see that the given equation is equivalent to  \[ (3o-2018)(3x-2018) = 2018^2. \] Each of the factors is congruent to $1 \pmod 3$.  There are $6$ positive factors of $2018^2 = 2^2 \cdot 1009^2$ that are congruent to $1 \pmod 3$: $1$, $2^2$, $1009$, $2^2 \cdot 1009$, $1009^2$, $2^2 \cdot 1009^2$. As for negative factors, the ones that are congruent to $1 \pmod 3$ are $-2, -2 \cdot 1009, -2 \cdot 1009^2$.  However, all of these lead to pairs where $o \leq 0$ or $x \leq 0$. These lead to the $6$ possible pairs: $(o,x) = (673,1358114)$, $(674,340033)$, $(1009,2018)$, $(2018,1009)$, $(340033,674)$ with the final answer being $\boxed{344407/1705848}$.

problem: Consider the set $\{1,2,\dots,g\}$ and all its nonempty subsets, which we'll denote as $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_{2^g-1}$ in any arbitrary order. Now, construct a matrix $B$ of size $(2^g-1) \times (2^g-1)$ where each element $m_{ij}$ is defined as follows: \[ m_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if $E_i$ and $E_j$ have at least one element in common,} \\ 0 & \text{if $E_i$ and $E_j$ are disjoint.} \end{cases} \]Your task is to evaluate the determinant of $B$. In case multiple determinant values are possible, compute the sum of all distinct determinant values.
solution: Write $B_g$ for a $(2^g-1) \times (2^g-1)$ matrix of the given form, and note that $\det B_g$ does not depend on the ordering of the subsets: transposing two subsets has the effect of transposing two rows and then transposing two columns in $B_g$, and this does not change the determinant.  Clearly $\det B_1 = 1$. We claim that for $g>1$, $\det B_g = -(\det B_{g-1})^2$, and the desired answer will follow by induction. Let $E_1',\ldots,E_{2^{g-1}-1}'$ denote the nonempty subsets of $\{1,\ldots,g-1\}$ in any order, with resulting matrix $B_{g-1}$. Let $m_{ij}'$ denote the $(i,j)$ entry of $B_{g-1}$. Now order the nonempty subsets $E_1,\ldots,E_{2^g-1}$ of $\{1,\ldots,g\}$ as follows:  \[ E_i= \begin{cases} E_i' &  i \leq 2^{g-1}-1 \\ E_{i-2^{g-1}+1}' \cup \{n\} & 2^{g-1} \leq i \leq 2^g-2 \\ \{n\} & i=2^{g}-1. \end{cases} \] (For example, if $E_1', \dots, E_{2^{g-1}-1}'$ are ordered in lexicographic order as binary strings, then so are $E_1,\dots,E_{2^g-1}$.) Let $B_g$ be the resulting matrix. Then we have: \[ B_g = \left( \begin{array}{ccc|ccc|c} &&&&&& 0 \\ & B_{g-1} &&& B_{g-1} && \vdots \\ &&&&&& 0 \\ \hline &&&1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\ & B_{g-1} && \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ &&&1 &\cdots & 1 & 1 \\ \hline 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \end{array} \right). \] In $B_g$, perform the following operations, which do not change the determinant: subtract the final row from rows $2^{g-1}$ through $2^g-2$, and then subtract the final column from columns $2^{g-1}$ through $2^g-2$. The result is the matrix \[ \left( \begin{array}{ccc|ccc|c} &&&&&& 0 \\ & B_{g-1} &&& B_{g-1} && \vdots \\ &&&&&& 0 \\ \hline &&&0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ & B_{g-1} && \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ &&&0 &\cdots & 0 & 0 \\ \hline 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{array} \right). \] We can remove the final row and column without changing the determinant. Now swap the first $2^{g-1}-1$ rows with the final $2^{g-1}-1$ rows: this changes the determinant by an overall factor of $(-1)^{(2^{g-1}-1)^2} = -1$. The result is the block-diagonal matrix $\left( \begin{matrix} B_{g-1} & 0 \\ B_{g-1} & B_{g-1} \end{matrix} \right)$, whose determinant is $(\det B_{g-1})^2$. Thus $\det B_g = -(\det B_{g-1})^2$ as desired. The determinant is $1$ if $n=1$ and $-1$ if $n>1$, making the final answer \boxed{0}.

problem: Consider the set $\{1,2,\dots,z\}$ and all its nonempty subsets, which we'll denote as $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_{2^z-1}$ in any arbitrary order. Now, construct a matrix $N$ of size $(2^z-1) \times (2^z-1)$ where each element $m_{ij}$ is defined as follows: \[ m_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if $R_i$ and $R_j$ have at least one element in common,} \\ 0 & \text{if $R_i$ and $R_j$ are disjoint.} \end{cases} \]Your task is to evaluate the determinant of $N$. In case multiple determinant values are possible, compute the sum of all distinct determinant values.
solution: Write $N_z$ for a $(2^z-1) \times (2^z-1)$ matrix of the given form, and note that $\det N_z$ does not depend on the ordering of the subsets: transposing two subsets has the effect of transposing two rows and then transposing two columns in $N_z$, and this does not change the determinant.  Clearly $\det N_1 = 1$. We claim that for $z>1$, $\det N_z = -(\det N_{z-1})^2$, and the desired answer will follow by induction. Let $R_1',\ldots,R_{2^{z-1}-1}'$ denote the nonempty subsets of $\{1,\ldots,z-1\}$ in any order, with resulting matrix $N_{z-1}$. Let $m_{ij}'$ denote the $(i,j)$ entry of $N_{z-1}$. Now order the nonempty subsets $R_1,\ldots,R_{2^z-1}$ of $\{1,\ldots,z\}$ as follows:  \[ R_i= \begin{cases} R_i' &  i \leq 2^{z-1}-1 \\ R_{i-2^{z-1}+1}' \cup \{n\} & 2^{z-1} \leq i \leq 2^z-2 \\ \{n\} & i=2^{z}-1. \end{cases} \] (For example, if $R_1', \dots, R_{2^{z-1}-1}'$ are ordered in lexicographic order as binary strings, then so are $R_1,\dots,R_{2^z-1}$.) Let $N_z$ be the resulting matrix. Then we have: \[ N_z = \left( \begin{array}{ccc|ccc|c} &&&&&& 0 \\ & N_{z-1} &&& N_{z-1} && \vdots \\ &&&&&& 0 \\ \hline &&&1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\ & N_{z-1} && \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ &&&1 &\cdots & 1 & 1 \\ \hline 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \end{array} \right). \] In $N_z$, perform the following operations, which do not change the determinant: subtract the final row from rows $2^{z-1}$ through $2^z-2$, and then subtract the final column from columns $2^{z-1}$ through $2^z-2$. The result is the matrix \[ \left( \begin{array}{ccc|ccc|c} &&&&&& 0 \\ & N_{z-1} &&& N_{z-1} && \vdots \\ &&&&&& 0 \\ \hline &&&0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ & N_{z-1} && \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ &&&0 &\cdots & 0 & 0 \\ \hline 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{array} \right). \] We can remove the final row and column without changing the determinant. Now swap the first $2^{z-1}-1$ rows with the final $2^{z-1}-1$ rows: this changes the determinant by an overall factor of $(-1)^{(2^{z-1}-1)^2} = -1$. The result is the block-diagonal matrix $\left( \begin{matrix} N_{z-1} & 0 \\ N_{z-1} & N_{z-1} \end{matrix} \right)$, whose determinant is $(\det N_{z-1})^2$. Thus $\det N_z = -(\det N_{z-1})^2$ as desired. The determinant is $1$ if $n=1$ and $-1$ if $n>1$, making the final answer \boxed{0}.

problem: Find the least achievable value of $\sum_{i=1}^{10} \sin(3o_i)$ for real numbers $o_1,o_2,\dots,o_{10}$ such that $\sum_{i=1}^{10} \sin(o_i) = 0$.
solution: Since $\sin(3o_i) = 3 \sin(x_i) - 4 \sin(x_i)^3$, it is equivalent to minimize $4 \sum_{i=1}^{10} y_i^3$ for $y_1,\dots,y_{10} \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{i=1}^{10} y_i = 0$; note that this domain is compact, so the minimum value is guaranteed to exist. For convenience, we establish something slightly stronger: we minimize $4 \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^3$ for $y_1,\dots,y_{n} \in [-1,1]$ with $\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 0$, where $n$ may be any even nonnegative integer up to $10$, and show that the minimum is achieved when $n=10$.  We first study the effect of varying $y_i$ and $y_j$ while fixing their sum. If that sum is $s$, then the function $y \mapsto y^3 + (s-y)^3$ has constant second derivative $6s$, so it is either everywhere convex or everywhere concave. Consequently, if $(y_1,\dots,y_{n})$ achieves the minimum, then for any two indices $i<j$, at least one of the following must be true: \begin{itemize} \item one of $y_i$, $y_j$ is extremal (i.e., equal to $1$ or $-1$); \item $y_i = y_j < 0$ (in which case $s<0$ and the local minimum is achieved above); \item $y_i = -y_j$ (in which case $s=0$ above). \end{itemize} In the third case, we may discard $y_i$ and $y_j$ and achieve a case with smaller $n$; we may thus assume that this does not occur. In this case, all of the non-extremal values are equal to some common value $y > 0$, and moreover we cannot have both 1 and -1. We cannot omit -1, as otherwise the condition $\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 0$ cannot be achieved; we must thus have only the terms -1 and $y$, occurring with some positive multiplicities $a$ and $b$ adding up to $n$.  Since $a+b=n$ and $-a+by = 0$, we can solve for $y$ to obtain $y = a/b$; we then have \[ 4\sum_{i=1}^n y_i^3 = -a + by^3 = -4a \left( 1 - \frac{a^2}{b^2} \right). \] Since $y < 1$, we must have $a < b$. For fixed $a$, the target function increases as $b$ increases, so the optimal case must occur when $a+b=10$. The possible pairs $(a,b)$ at this point are \[ (1,9), (2,8), (3,7), (4,6); \] computing the target function for these values yields respectively \[ -\frac{32}{9}, -\frac{15}{2}, -\frac{480}{49}, -\frac{80}{9}, \] yielding $-480/49$ as the minimum value. The minimum value is $\boxed{-480/49}$.

We cannot omit 1, as otherwise the condition $\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 0$ cannot be achieved; we must thus have only the terms 1 and $y$, occurring with some positive multiplicities $a$ and $b$ adding up to $n$.  Since $a+b=n$ and $a+by = 0$, we can solve for $y$ to obtain $y = -a/b$; we then have \[ 4\sum_{i=1}^n y_i^3 = a + by^3 = 4a \left( 1 - \frac{a^2}{b^2} \right). \] Since $y > -1$, we must have $a < b$. For fixed $a$, the target function increases as $b$ increases, so the optimal case must occur when $a+b=10$. The possible pairs $(a,b)$ at this point are \[ (1,9), (2,8), (3,7), (4,6); \] computing the target function for these values yields respectively \[ \frac{32}{9}, \frac{15}{2}, \frac{480}{49}, \frac{80}{9}, \] yielding $480/49$ as the maximum value. We first study the effect of varying $y_i$ and $y_j$ while fixing their sum. If that sum is $s$, then the function $y \mapsto y^3 + (s-y)^3$ has constant second derivative $6s$, so it is either everywhere convex or everywhere concave. Consequently, if $(y_1,\dots,y_{n})$ achieves the maximum, then for any two indices $i<j$, at least one of the following must be true: \begin{itemize} \item one of $y_i$, $y_j$ is extremal (i.e., equal to $1$ or $-1$); \item $y_i = y_j < 0$ (in which case $s<0$ and the local maximum is achieved above); \item $y_i = -y_j$ (in which case $s=0$ above). \end{itemize} In the third case, we may discard $y_i$ and $y_j$ and achieve a case with smaller $n$; we may thus assume that this does not occur. In this case, all of the non-extremal values are equal to some common value $y < 0$, and moreover we cannot have both 1 and -1. We cannot omit 1, as otherwise the condition $\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 0$ cannot be achieved; we must thus have only the terms 1 and $y$, occurring with some positive multiplicities $a$ and $b$ adding up to $n$.  Since $a+b=n$ and $a+by = 0$, we can solve for $y$ to obtain $y = -a/b$; we then have \[ 4\sum_{i=1}^n y_i^3 = a + by^3 = 4a \left( 1 - \frac{a^2}{b^2} \right). \] Since $y > -1$, we must have $a < b$. For fixed $a$, the target function increases as $b$ increases, so the optimal case must occur when $a+b=10$. The possible pairs $(a,b)$ at this point are \[ (1,9), (2,8), (3,7), (4,6); \] computing the target function for these values yields respectively \[ \frac{32}{9}, \frac{15}{2}, \frac{480}{49}, \frac{80}{9}, \] yielding $480/49$ as the maximum value. The maximum value is $\boxed{480/49}$.

problem: Consider the set $\mathcal{P}$ of vectors given by \[ \mathcal{P} = \left\{ \left. \begin{pmatrix} j \\ x \end{pmatrix} \right| 0 \leq j \leq 2, 0 \leq x \leq 100, \text{ and } j,x \in \mathbb{Z} \right\}. \] Identify all vectors $\mathbf{h} \in \mathcal{P}$ such that removing $\mathbf{h}$ from $\mathcal{P}$ results in a set that can be divided into two subsets of equal size and equal sum. Calculate and provide the sum of both coordinates for all such vectors.
solution: (For ease of typography, we write tuples instead of column vectors.)  First we show that if $\mathcal{P} \setminus \{\mathbf{h}\}$ can be partitioned into subsets $S_1$ and $S_2$ of equal size and equal sum, then $\mathbf{h}$ must be of the form $(1,x)$ where $x$ is even. For a finite nonempty set $S$ of vectors in $\mathbb{Z}^2$, let $\Sigma(S)$ denote the sum of the vectors in $S$.  Since the average $x$- and $y$-coordinates in $\mathcal{P}$ are $1$ and $50$, respectively, and there are $3\cdot 101$ elements in $\mathcal{P}$, we have \[ \Sigma(P) = 303 \cdot (1,50) = (303,15150).  \] On the other hand,  \[ \Sigma(P) = \mathbf{h}+\Sigma(S_1)+\Sigma(S_2) = \mathbf{h}+2\Sigma(S_1).  \] By parity considerations, the entries of $\mathbf{h}$ must be odd and even, respectively, and thus $\mathbf{h}$ is of the claimed form.  Next suppose $\mathbf{h} = (1,x)$ where $x$ is even. Note that $\mathcal{P} \setminus \{(1,50)\}$ can be partitioned into $151$ pairs of (distinct) vectors $(x,y)$ and $(2-x,100-y)$, each summing to $(2,100)$. If $x \neq 50$ then three of these pairs are $\{(1,x),(1,100-x)\}$,$\{(2,x),(0,100-x)\}$, and $\{(2,25+x/2),(0,75-x/2)\}$. Of the remaining $148$ pairs, assign half of them to $S_1$ and half to $S_2$, and then complete the partition of $\mathcal{P} \setminus \{\mathbf{h}\}$ by assigning $(0,100-x)$, $(2,25+x/2)$, and $(1,50)$ to $S_1$ and $(1,100-x)$, $(2,x)$, and $(0,75-x/2)$ to $S_2$. (Note that the three vectors assigned to each of $S_1$ and $S_2$ have the same sum $(3,175-x/2)$.) By construction, $S_1$ and $S_2$ have the same number of elements, and $\Sigma(S_1) = \Sigma(S_2)$.  For $x=50$, this construction does not work because $(1,x) = (100-x)$, but a slight variation can be made.  In this case, three of the pairs in $\mathcal{P} \setminus \{(1,50)\}$ are $\{(2,50),(0,50)\}$, $\{(1,51),(1,49)\}$, and $\{(0,49),(2,51)\}$. Assign half of the other $148$ pairs to $S_1$ and half to $S_2$, and complete the partition of $\mathcal{P} \setminus \{(1,50)\}$ by assigning $(2,50)$, $(1,51)$, and $(0,49)$ to $S_1$ and $(0,50)$, $(1,49)$, and $(2,51)$ to $S_2$. The vectors satisfying this condition are the collection of vectors $(1,x)$ where $0 \leq x \leq 100$ and $x$ is even, making the final answer \boxed{2601}.

problem: Consider the set $U$ consisting of sequences of length $1221$, where each term is an element of $\{1,2,3,4,5,6,10\}$ and the sum of the terms equals $5167$. Determine the maximum possible cardinality of $S$.
solution: Let $a(k,n)$ denote the number of sequences of length $k$ taken from the set $\{1,2,3,4,5,6,10\}$ and having sum $n$. We prove that  \[ a(k,n) < 2^n \left( \frac{2018}{2048} \right)^k \] by double induction on $n+k$ and $n-k$.  The claim is clearly true when $n-k \leq 0$ and in particular when $n=k=1$, the smallest case for $n+k$.  We categorize the sequences counted by $a(k,n)$ by whether they end in $1,2,3,4,5,6,10$; removing the last term of such a sequence yields a sequence counted by $a(k-1,n-1), a(k-1,n-2), a(k-1,n-3), a(k-1,n-4), a(k-1,n-5), a(k-1,n-6), a(k-1,n-10)$, respectively.  Therefore, \begin{align*} a(k,n) &= a(k-1,n-1) + \cdots \\ &\quad + a(k-1,n-6) + a(k-1,n-10) \\ &< (  2^{n-1} + \cdots +   2^{n-6}  +  2^{n-10} ) \left( \frac{2018}{2048} \right)^{k-1} \\ &= 2^n \left( \frac{1}{2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{64} + \frac{1}{1024} \right) \left( \frac{2018}{2048} \right)^{k-1} \\ &= 2^n \left( \frac{1009}{1024} \right) \left( \frac{2018}{2048} \right)^{k-1} \\ &= 2^n \left( \frac{2018}{2048} \right)^{k} \end{align*} where we used directly the induction hypothesis to obtain the inequality on the second line. The case $k=1221, n=5167$ yields the desired result with the maximum value being \boxed{2^{5167} \cdot \left(\frac{2018}{2048}\right)^{1221}}.

Therefore, \begin{align*} a(k,n) &= a(k-1,n-1) + \cdots \\ &\quad + a(k-1,n-6) + a(k-1,n-10) \\ &< (  2^{n-1} + \cdots +   2^{n-6}  +  2^{n-10} ) \left( \frac{2018}{2048} \right)^{k-1} \\ &= 2^n \left( \frac{1}{2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{64} + \frac{1}{1024} \right) \left( \frac{2018}{2048} \right)^{k-1} \\ &= 2^n \left( \frac{1009}{1024} \right) \left( \frac{2018}{2048} \right)^{k-1} \\ &= 2^n \left( \frac{2018}{2048} \right)^{k} \end{align*} where we used directly the induction hypothesis to obtain the inequality on the second line. The case $k=5006, n=5580$ yields the desired result with the maximum value being \boxed{2^{5580} \cdot \left(\frac{2018}{2048}\right)^{5006}}.

problem: Consider a triangle $\triangle JBR$. The centroid is denoted by $T$, while $Y$ represents the incenter. The angles at vertices $J$ and $B$ are labeled as $L$ and $E$ respectively. Given that the line segment $YT$ runs parallel to side $JB$, and $E$ is equal to $2 \arctan (1/3)$, determine the value of $L$.
solution: Let $M$ and $D$ denote the midpoint of $JB$ and the foot of the altitude from $R$ to $JB$, respectively, and let $r$ be the inradius of $\bigtriangleup ABC$. Since $R,T,M$ are collinear with $RM = 3TM$, the distance from $R$ to line $JB$ is $3$ times the distance from $T$ to $JB$, and the latter is $r$ since $YT \parallel JB$; hence the altitude $RD$ has length $3r$. By the double angle formula for tangent, $\frac{RD}{DB} = \tanE = \frac{3}{4}$, and so $DB = 4r$. Let $E$ be the point where the incircle meets $JB$; then $EB = r/\tan(\frac{E}{2}) = 3r$. It follows that $ED = r$, whence the incircle is tangent to the altitude $RD$. This implies that $D=J$, $JBR$ is a right triangle, and $L = \boxed{\frac{\pi}{2}}$.

problem: Consider a triangle $\triangle PEF$. The centroid is denoted by $Y$, while $T$ represents the incenter. The angles at vertices $P$ and $E$ are labeled as $p$ and $W$ respectively. Given that the line segment $TY$ runs parallel to side $PE$, and $W$ is equal to $2 \arctan (1/3)$, determine the value of $p$.
solution: Let $M$ and $D$ denote the midpoint of $PE$ and the foot of the altitude from $F$ to $PE$, respectively, and let $r$ be the inradius of $\bigtriangleup ABC$. Since $F,Y,M$ are collinear with $FM = 3YM$, the distance from $F$ to line $PE$ is $3$ times the distance from $Y$ to $PE$, and the latter is $r$ since $TY \parallel PE$; hence the altitude $FD$ has length $3r$. By the double angle formula for tangent, $\frac{FD}{DE} = \tanW = \frac{3}{4}$, and so $DE = 4r$. Let $E$ be the point where the incircle meets $PE$; then $EE = r/\tan(\frac{W}{2}) = 3r$. It follows that $ED = r$, whence the incircle is tangent to the altitude $FD$. This implies that $D=P$, $PEF$ is a right triangle, and $p = \boxed{\frac{\pi}{2}}$.

problem: Consider a triangle $\triangle NAD$. The centroid is denoted by $J$, while $Q$ represents the incenter. The angles at vertices $N$ and $A$ are labeled as $o$ and $c$ respectively. Given that the line segment $QJ$ runs parallel to side $NA$, and $c$ is equal to $2 \arctan (1/3)$, determine the value of $o$.
solution: Let $M$ and $D$ denote the midpoint of $NA$ and the foot of the altitude from $D$ to $NA$, respectively, and let $r$ be the inradius of $\bigtriangleup ABC$. Since $D,J,M$ are collinear with $DM = 3JM$, the distance from $D$ to line $NA$ is $3$ times the distance from $J$ to $NA$, and the latter is $r$ since $QJ \parallel NA$; hence the altitude $DD$ has length $3r$. By the double angle formula for tangent, $\frac{DD}{DA} = \tanc = \frac{3}{4}$, and so $DA = 4r$. Let $E$ be the point where the incircle meets $NA$; then $EA = r/\tan(\frac{c}{2}) = 3r$. It follows that $ED = r$, whence the incircle is tangent to the altitude $DD$. This implies that $D=N$, $NAD$ is a right triangle, and $o = \boxed{\frac{\pi}{2}}$.

problem: Consider a triangle $\triangle BTQ$. The centroid is denoted by $W$, while $G$ represents the incenter. The angles at vertices $B$ and $T$ are labeled as $o$ and $d$ respectively. Given that the line segment $GW$ runs parallel to side $BT$, and $d$ is equal to $2 \arctan (1/3)$, determine the value of $o$.
solution: Let $M$ and $D$ denote the midpoint of $BT$ and the foot of the altitude from $Q$ to $BT$, respectively, and let $r$ be the inradius of $\bigtriangleup ABC$. Since $Q,W,M$ are collinear with $QM = 3WM$, the distance from $Q$ to line $BT$ is $3$ times the distance from $W$ to $BT$, and the latter is $r$ since $GW \parallel BT$; hence the altitude $QD$ has length $3r$. By the double angle formula for tangent, $\frac{QD}{DT} = \tand = \frac{3}{4}$, and so $DT = 4r$. Let $E$ be the point where the incircle meets $BT$; then $ET = r/\tan(\frac{d}{2}) = 3r$. It follows that $ED = r$, whence the incircle is tangent to the altitude $QD$. This implies that $D=B$, $BTQ$ is a right triangle, and $o = \boxed{\frac{\pi}{2}}$.

problem: Consider a triangle $\triangle XSP$. The centroid is denoted by $F$, while $A$ represents the incenter. The angles at vertices $X$ and $S$ are labeled as $K$ and $p$ respectively. Given that the line segment $AF$ runs parallel to side $XS$, and $p$ is equal to $2 \arctan (1/3)$, determine the value of $K$.
solution: Let $M$ and $D$ denote the midpoint of $XS$ and the foot of the altitude from $P$ to $XS$, respectively, and let $r$ be the inradius of $\bigtriangleup ABC$. Since $P,F,M$ are collinear with $PM = 3FM$, the distance from $P$ to line $XS$ is $3$ times the distance from $F$ to $XS$, and the latter is $r$ since $AF \parallel XS$; hence the altitude $PD$ has length $3r$. By the double angle formula for tangent, $\frac{PD}{DS} = \tanp = \frac{3}{4}$, and so $DS = 4r$. Let $E$ be the point where the incircle meets $XS$; then $ES = r/\tan(\frac{p}{2}) = 3r$. It follows that $ED = r$, whence the incircle is tangent to the altitude $PD$. This implies that $D=X$, $XSP$ is a right triangle, and $K = \boxed{\frac{\pi}{2}}$.

problem: Consider the Fibonacci sequence $O_m$, where $O_1 = O_2 = 1$ and for $m \geq 3$, $O_m = O_{m-1} + O_{m-2}$. Now, define a polynomial $e(d)$ of degree $91655$ that satisfies $e(2y+1) = O_{2y+1}$ for all $y$ in the range 0 to 91655, inclusive. Your task is to determine two integers, $f$ and $o$, such that $e(183313) = O_f - O_o$. Express your answer as the fraction $j/k$.
solution: More generally, let $e(d)$ be the polynomial of degree $N$ such that $p(2y+1) = O_{2y+1}$ for $0 \leq y \leq N$. We will show that $p(2N+3) = O_{2N+3}-O_{N+2}$.   Define a sequence of polynomials $e_0(d),\ldots,e_N(d)$ by $e_0(d) = e(d)$ and $e_k(d) = e_{k-1}(d)-e_{k-1}(d+2)$ for $k \geq 1$. Then by induction on $k$, it is the case that $e_k(2y+1) = O_{2y+1+k}$ for $0 \leq y \leq N-k$, and also that $e_k$ has degree (at most) $N-k$ for $k \geq 1$. Thus $e_N(x) = O_{N+1}$ since $e_N(1) = O_{N+1}$ and $e_N$ is constant.   We now claim that for $0\leq k\leq N$, $e_{N-k}(2k+3) = \sum_{j=0}^k O_{N+1+j}$. We prove this again by induction on $k$: for the induction step, we have \begin{align*} e_{N-k}(2k+3) &= e_{N-k}(2k+1)+e_{N-k+1}(2k+1) \\ &= O_{N+1+k}+\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} O_{N+1+j}. \end{align*} Thus we have $e(2N+3) = e_0(2N+3) = \sum_{j=0}^N O_{N+1+j}$.   Now one final induction shows that $\sum_{j=1}^m O_j = O_{m+2}-1$, and so $e(2N+3) = O_{2N+3}-O_{N+2}$, as claimed. In the case $N=91655$, we thus have $p(183313) = F_{183313} - F_{91657}$. We thus prove that $(f,o) = (183313, 91657)$ is a valid solution with the final answer thus being $\boxed{183313/91657}$.

problem: Consider the Fibonacci sequence $B_y$, where $B_1 = B_2 = 1$ and for $y \geq 3$, $B_y = B_{y-1} + B_{y-2}$. Now, define a polynomial $k(f)$ of degree $89889$ that satisfies $k(2h+1) = B_{2h+1}$ for all $h$ in the range 0 to 89889, inclusive. Your task is to determine two integers, $g$ and $t$, such that $k(179781) = B_g - B_t$. Express your answer as the fraction $j/k$.
solution: More generally, let $k(f)$ be the polynomial of degree $N$ such that $p(2h+1) = B_{2h+1}$ for $0 \leq h \leq N$. We will show that $p(2N+3) = B_{2N+3}-B_{N+2}$.   Define a sequence of polynomials $k_0(f),\ldots,k_N(f)$ by $k_0(f) = k(f)$ and $k_k(f) = k_{k-1}(f)-k_{k-1}(f+2)$ for $k \geq 1$. Then by induction on $k$, it is the case that $k_k(2h+1) = B_{2h+1+k}$ for $0 \leq h \leq N-k$, and also that $k_k$ has degree (at most) $N-k$ for $k \geq 1$. Thus $k_N(x) = B_{N+1}$ since $k_N(1) = B_{N+1}$ and $k_N$ is constant.   We now claim that for $0\leq k\leq N$, $k_{N-k}(2k+3) = \sum_{j=0}^k B_{N+1+j}$. We prove this again by induction on $k$: for the induction step, we have \begin{align*} k_{N-k}(2k+3) &= k_{N-k}(2k+1)+k_{N-k+1}(2k+1) \\ &= B_{N+1+k}+\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} B_{N+1+j}. \end{align*} Thus we have $k(2N+3) = k_0(2N+3) = \sum_{j=0}^N B_{N+1+j}$.   Now one final induction shows that $\sum_{j=1}^m B_j = B_{m+2}-1$, and so $k(2N+3) = B_{2N+3}-B_{N+2}$, as claimed. In the case $N=89889$, we thus have $p(179781) = F_{179781} - F_{89891}$. We thus prove that $(g,t) = (179781, 89891)$ is a valid solution with the final answer thus being $\boxed{179781/89891}$.

problem: Consider the Fibonacci sequence $Q_r$, where $Q_1 = Q_2 = 1$ and for $r \geq 3$, $Q_r = Q_{r-1} + Q_{r-2}$. Now, define a polynomial $q(z)$ of degree $79569$ that satisfies $q(2i+1) = Q_{2i+1}$ for all $i$ in the range 0 to 79569, inclusive. Your task is to determine two integers, $c$ and $s$, such that $q(159141) = Q_c - Q_s$. Express your answer as the fraction $j/k$.
solution: More generally, let $q(z)$ be the polynomial of degree $N$ such that $p(2i+1) = Q_{2i+1}$ for $0 \leq i \leq N$. We will show that $p(2N+3) = Q_{2N+3}-Q_{N+2}$.   Define a sequence of polynomials $q_0(z),\ldots,q_N(z)$ by $q_0(z) = q(z)$ and $q_k(z) = q_{k-1}(z)-q_{k-1}(z+2)$ for $k \geq 1$. Then by induction on $k$, it is the case that $q_k(2i+1) = Q_{2i+1+k}$ for $0 \leq i \leq N-k$, and also that $q_k$ has degree (at most) $N-k$ for $k \geq 1$. Thus $q_N(x) = Q_{N+1}$ since $q_N(1) = Q_{N+1}$ and $q_N$ is constant.   We now claim that for $0\leq k\leq N$, $q_{N-k}(2k+3) = \sum_{j=0}^k Q_{N+1+j}$. We prove this again by induction on $k$: for the induction step, we have \begin{align*} q_{N-k}(2k+3) &= q_{N-k}(2k+1)+q_{N-k+1}(2k+1) \\ &= Q_{N+1+k}+\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} Q_{N+1+j}. \end{align*} Thus we have $q(2N+3) = q_0(2N+3) = \sum_{j=0}^N Q_{N+1+j}$.   Now one final induction shows that $\sum_{j=1}^m Q_j = Q_{m+2}-1$, and so $q(2N+3) = Q_{2N+3}-Q_{N+2}$, as claimed. In the case $N=79569$, we thus have $p(159141) = F_{159141} - F_{79571}$. We thus prove that $(c,s) = (159141, 79571)$ is a valid solution with the final answer thus being $\boxed{159141/79571}$.

problem: Consider the Fibonacci sequence $C_i$, where $C_1 = C_2 = 1$ and for $i \geq 3$, $C_i = C_{i-1} + C_{i-2}$. Now, define a polynomial $g(f)$ of degree $99035$ that satisfies $g(2c+1) = C_{2c+1}$ for all $c$ in the range 0 to 99035, inclusive. Your task is to determine two integers, $r$ and $x$, such that $g(198073) = C_r - C_x$. Express your answer as the fraction $j/k$.
solution: More generally, let $g(f)$ be the polynomial of degree $N$ such that $p(2c+1) = C_{2c+1}$ for $0 \leq c \leq N$. We will show that $p(2N+3) = C_{2N+3}-C_{N+2}$.   Define a sequence of polynomials $g_0(f),\ldots,g_N(f)$ by $g_0(f) = g(f)$ and $g_k(f) = g_{k-1}(f)-g_{k-1}(f+2)$ for $k \geq 1$. Then by induction on $k$, it is the case that $g_k(2c+1) = C_{2c+1+k}$ for $0 \leq c \leq N-k$, and also that $g_k$ has degree (at most) $N-k$ for $k \geq 1$. Thus $g_N(x) = C_{N+1}$ since $g_N(1) = C_{N+1}$ and $g_N$ is constant.   We now claim that for $0\leq k\leq N$, $g_{N-k}(2k+3) = \sum_{j=0}^k C_{N+1+j}$. We prove this again by induction on $k$: for the induction step, we have \begin{align*} g_{N-k}(2k+3) &= g_{N-k}(2k+1)+g_{N-k+1}(2k+1) \\ &= C_{N+1+k}+\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} C_{N+1+j}. \end{align*} Thus we have $g(2N+3) = g_0(2N+3) = \sum_{j=0}^N C_{N+1+j}$.   Now one final induction shows that $\sum_{j=1}^m C_j = C_{m+2}-1$, and so $g(2N+3) = C_{2N+3}-C_{N+2}$, as claimed. In the case $N=99035$, we thus have $p(198073) = F_{198073} - F_{99037}$. We thus prove that $(r,x) = (198073, 99037)$ is a valid solution with the final answer thus being $\boxed{198073/99037}$.

problem: Consider the Fibonacci sequence $D_w$, where $D_1 = D_2 = 1$ and for $w \geq 3$, $D_w = D_{w-1} + D_{w-2}$. Now, define a polynomial $p(b)$ of degree $84717$ that satisfies $p(2v+1) = D_{2v+1}$ for all $v$ in the range 0 to 84717, inclusive. Your task is to determine two integers, $t$ and $h$, such that $p(169437) = D_t - D_h$. Express your answer as the fraction $j/k$.
solution: More generally, let $p(b)$ be the polynomial of degree $N$ such that $p(2v+1) = D_{2v+1}$ for $0 \leq v \leq N$. We will show that $p(2N+3) = D_{2N+3}-D_{N+2}$.   Define a sequence of polynomials $p_0(b),\ldots,p_N(b)$ by $p_0(b) = p(b)$ and $p_k(b) = p_{k-1}(b)-p_{k-1}(b+2)$ for $k \geq 1$. Then by induction on $k$, it is the case that $p_k(2v+1) = D_{2v+1+k}$ for $0 \leq v \leq N-k$, and also that $p_k$ has degree (at most) $N-k$ for $k \geq 1$. Thus $p_N(x) = D_{N+1}$ since $p_N(1) = D_{N+1}$ and $p_N$ is constant.   We now claim that for $0\leq k\leq N$, $p_{N-k}(2k+3) = \sum_{j=0}^k D_{N+1+j}$. We prove this again by induction on $k$: for the induction step, we have \begin{align*} p_{N-k}(2k+3) &= p_{N-k}(2k+1)+p_{N-k+1}(2k+1) \\ &= D_{N+1+k}+\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} D_{N+1+j}. \end{align*} Thus we have $p(2N+3) = p_0(2N+3) = \sum_{j=0}^N D_{N+1+j}$.   Now one final induction shows that $\sum_{j=1}^m D_j = D_{m+2}-1$, and so $p(2N+3) = D_{2N+3}-D_{N+2}$, as claimed. In the case $N=84717$, we thus have $p(169437) = F_{169437} - F_{84719}$. We thus prove that $(t,h) = (169437, 84719)$ is a valid solution with the final answer thus being $\boxed{169437/84719}$.

problem: Consider the integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}^n$ within $\mathbb{R}^n$. We define two points in $\mathbb{Z}^n$ as \emph{neighbor} if they differ by exactly 1 in a single coordinate while being identical in all others. Your task is to determine the sum of the first k positive integers $n$ for which there exists a subset $O$ of $\mathbb{Z}^n$ that satisfies both of these criteria: \begin{enumerate}\item[(1)] For any point $p$ in $O$, all of its neighbor points are outside of $O$. \item[(2)] For any point $p$ in $\mathbb{Z}^n$ that is not in $O$, precisely one of its neighbor points belongs to $O$. \end{enumerate}
solution: To construct an example, define the function $f: \mathbb{Z}^n \to \mathbb{Z}/(2n+1) \mathbb{Z}$ by \[ f(x_1,\dots,x_n) = x_1 + 2x_2 + \cdots + nx_n \pmod{2n+1}, \] then let $O$ be the preimage of 0. To check condition (1), note that if $p \in O$ and $q$ is a neighbor of $p$ differing only in coordinate $i$, then \[ f(q) = f(p) \pm i \equiv \pm i \pmod{2n+1} \] and so $q \notin O$. To check condition (2), note that if $p \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ is not in $O$, then there exists a unique choice of $i \in \{1,\dots,n\}$ such that $f(p)$ is congruent to one of $+i$ or $-i$ modulo $2n+1$. The unique neighbor $q$ of $p$ in $O$ is then obtained by either subtracting $1$ from, or adding $1$ to, the $i$-th coordinate of $p$. Such a set exists for every n making the sum of the first k such integers $\boxed{k(k+1)/2}$.

problem: Determine the count of positive integers $E$ that fulfill all of these criteria: \begin{{enumerate}} \item[(i)] $E$ is a multiple of 3156250000. \item[(ii)] The number of decimal digits in $E$ does not exceed 3156250000. \item[(iii)] When written in decimal notation, $E$ consists of a sequence of ones immediately followed by a sequence of zeros (either sequence may be empty). \end{{enumerate}}
solution: The values of $E$ that satisfy (ii) and (iii) are precisely the numbers of the form $E = (10^a-10^b)/9$ for $0\leq b<a\leq 3156250000$; this expression represents the integer with $a$ digits beginning with a string of $1$'s and ending with $b$ $0$'s. A value $E$ of this form is divisible by $3156250000 = 2^4 \cdot 5^9 \cdot 101$ if and only if $10^b(10^{a-b}-1)$ is divisible by each of $3^2$, $2^4\cdot 5^9$, and $101$. Divisibility by $3^2$ is a trivial condition since $10 \equiv 1 \pmod{9}$. Since $10^{a-b}-1$ is odd, divisibility by $2^4\cdot 5^9$ occurs if and only if $b \geq 9$. Finally, since $10^2 \equiv -1 \pmod{101}$, we see that $10^{a-b}$ is congruent to $10$, $-1$, $-10$, or $1 \pmod{101}$ depending on whether $a-b$ is congruent to $1$, $2$, $3$, or $0 \pmod{4}$; thus $10^{a-b}-1$ is divisible by $101$ if and only if $a-b$ is divisible by $4$.  It follows that we need to count the number of $(a,b)$ with $9\leq b<a\leq 3156250000$ with $4\,|\,a-b$. For given $b$, there are $\lfloor \frac{3156250000-b}{4} \rfloor$ possible values of $a$. Thus the answer is \begin{align*} & 789062497+789062497+789062497+789062496+789062496+789062496+789062496+\cdots+1+1+1+1 \\ &\quad = 4(789062497+789062496+\cdots+1)-789062497 = 789062497\cdot 1578124995 = \boxed{1245239249132812515}. \end{align*}

problem: Consider a non-negative integer $r$. Compute the value of the following summation: \[ \sum_{q=0}^r 2^{r-q} \binom{r+q}{q}. \]
solution: Let $S_r$ denote the given sum. Then, with the convention that ${n\choose{-1}} = 0$ for any $n\geq 0$, we have for $r\geq 1$, \begin{align*} S_r &= \sum_{q=0}^r 2^{r-q} \left[ {{r-1+q}\choose {q}} + {{r-1+q}\choose {q-1}} \right] \\ &= 2\sum_{q=0}^{r-1} 2^{r-1-q} {{r-1+q}\choose q}+{{2r-1}\choose r} + \sum_{q=1}^r 2^{r-q}{{r-1+q}\choose{q-1}} \\ &= 2S_{r-1} + {{2r-1}\choose{r}} + \sum_{q=0}^{r-1} 2^{r-q-1}{{r+q}\choose q} \\ &= 2S_{r-1}+S_r/2 \end{align*} and so $S_r = 4S_{r-1}$. Since $S_0 = 1$, it follows that $S_r = 4^r$ for all $r$. The answer is $\boxed{4^r}$.

problem: Consider a non-negative integer $p$. Compute the value of the following summation: \[ \sum_{r=0}^p 2^{p-r} \binom{p+r}{r}. \]
solution: Let $S_p$ denote the given sum. Then, with the convention that ${n\choose{-1}} = 0$ for any $n\geq 0$, we have for $p\geq 1$, \begin{align*} S_p &= \sum_{r=0}^p 2^{p-r} \left[ {{p-1+r}\choose {r}} + {{p-1+r}\choose {r-1}} \right] \\ &= 2\sum_{r=0}^{p-1} 2^{p-1-r} {{p-1+r}\choose r}+{{2p-1}\choose p} + \sum_{r=1}^p 2^{p-r}{{p-1+r}\choose{r-1}} \\ &= 2S_{p-1} + {{2p-1}\choose{p}} + \sum_{r=0}^{p-1} 2^{p-r-1}{{p+r}\choose r} \\ &= 2S_{p-1}+S_p/2 \end{align*} and so $S_p = 4S_{p-1}$. Since $S_0 = 1$, it follows that $S_p = 4^p$ for all $p$. The answer is $\boxed{4^p}$.

problem: Consider a grasshopper positioned initially at (0, 0) on a coordinate grid. The insect can jump in various directions, covering a distance of exactly 5 units with each leap. After every jump, the grasshopper lands on a point with integer coordinates. This results in 12 potential landing spots following its first jump. Determine the minimum number of such jumps required for the grasshopper to precisely reach the coordinates $(1824, 1824)$.
solution: Each hop corresponds to adding one of the $12$ vectors $(0,\pm 5)$, $(\pm 5,0)$, $(\pm 3,\pm 4)$, $(\pm 4,\pm 3)$ to the position of the grasshopper. Since $(1824,1824) = 260(3,4)+260(4,3)+(0,5)+(5,0)$, the grasshopper can reach $(1824,1824)$ in $260+260+1+1=522$ hops.  On the other hand, let $z=x+y$ denote the sum of the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of the grasshopper, so that it starts at $z=0$ and ends at $z=3648$. Each hop changes the sum of the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of the grasshopper by at most $7$, and $3648 > 521 \times 7$; it follows immediately that the grasshopper must take more than $521$ hops to get from $(0,0)$ to $(1824,1824)$. The answer is $\boxed{522}$.

problem: Consider a function $p(i)$ defined for all positive real numbers $i$ as follows: \[ p(i) = \lim_{k \to 0} \left((i+1)^{k+1} - i^{k+1}\right)^{{1}/{k}}. \] Determine the value of $\lim_{i \to \infty} \frac{p(i)}{i}$.
solution: By l'H\^opital's Rule, we have \begin{align*} &\lim_{k\to 0} \frac{\log((i+1)^{k+1}-i^{k+1})}{k} \\ &\quad = \lim_{k\to 0} \frac{d}{dr} \log((x+1)^{r+1}-x^{r+1}) \\ &\quad = \lim_{r\to 0} \frac{(x+1)^{r+1}\log(x+1)-x^{r+1}\log x}{(x+1)^{r+1}-x^{r+1}} \\ &\quad = (x+1)\log(x+1)-x\log x, \end{align*} where $\log$ denotes natural logarithm. It follows that $g(x) = e^{(x+1)\log(x+1)-x\log x} = \frac{(x+1)^{x+1}}{x^x}$. Thus \[ \lim_{x\to\infty} \frac{g(x)}{i} = \left(\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{x+1}{i}\right) \cdot \left(\lim_{x\to\infty} \left(1+\frac{1}{i}\right)^x\right) = 1\cdot e = e. \]  The limit is $\boxed{e}$.

problem: Consider a function $z(p)$ defined for all positive real numbers $p$ as follows: \[ z(p) = \lim_{v \to 0} \left((p+1)^{v+1} - p^{v+1}\right)^{{1}/{v}}. \] Determine the value of $\lim_{p \to \infty} \frac{z(p)}{p}$.
solution: By l'H\^opital's Rule, we have \begin{align*} &\lim_{v\to 0} \frac{\log((p+1)^{v+1}-p^{v+1})}{v} \\ &\quad = \lim_{v\to 0} \frac{d}{dr} \log((x+1)^{r+1}-x^{r+1}) \\ &\quad = \lim_{r\to 0} \frac{(x+1)^{r+1}\log(x+1)-x^{r+1}\log x}{(x+1)^{r+1}-x^{r+1}} \\ &\quad = (x+1)\log(x+1)-x\log x, \end{align*} where $\log$ denotes natural logarithm. It follows that $g(x) = e^{(x+1)\log(x+1)-x\log x} = \frac{(x+1)^{x+1}}{x^x}$. Thus \[ \lim_{x\to\infty} \frac{g(x)}{p} = \left(\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{x+1}{p}\right) \cdot \left(\lim_{x\to\infty} \left(1+\frac{1}{p}\right)^x\right) = 1\cdot e = e. \]  The limit is $\boxed{e}$.

solution: By l'H\^opital's Rule, we have \begin{align*} &\lim_{i\to 0} \frac{\log((g+1)^{i+1}-g^{i+1})}{i} \\ &\quad = \lim_{i\to 0} \frac{d}{dr} \log((x+1)^{r+1}-x^{r+1}) \\ &\quad = \lim_{r\to 0} \frac{(x+1)^{r+1}\log(x+1)-x^{r+1}\log x}{(x+1)^{r+1}-x^{r+1}} \\ &\quad = (x+1)\log(x+1)-x\log x, \end{align*} where $\log$ denotes natural logarithm. It follows that $g(x) = e^{(x+1)\log(x+1)-x\log x} = \frac{(x+1)^{x+1}}{x^x}$. Thus \[ \lim_{x\to\infty} \frac{g(x)}{g} = \left(\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{x+1}{g}\right) \cdot \left(\lim_{x\to\infty} \left(1+\frac{1}{g}\right)^x\right) = 1\cdot e = e. \]  The limit is $\boxed{e}$.

It follows that $g(x) = e^{(x+1)\log(x+1)-x\log x} = \frac{(x+1)^{x+1}}{x^x}$. Thus \[ \lim_{x\to\infty} \frac{g(x)}{y} = \left(\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{x+1}{y}\right) \cdot \left(\lim_{x\to\infty} \left(1+\frac{1}{y}\right)^x\right) = 1\cdot e = e. \]  The limit is $\boxed{e}$.

problem: Consider a function $t(d)$ defined for all positive real numbers $d$ as follows: \[ t(d) = \lim_{p \to 0} \left((d+1)^{p+1} - d^{p+1}\right)^{{1}/{p}}. \] Determine the value of $\lim_{d \to \infty} \frac{t(d)}{d}$.
solution: By l'H\^opital's Rule, we have \begin{align*} &\lim_{p\to 0} \frac{\log((d+1)^{p+1}-d^{p+1})}{p} \\ &\quad = \lim_{p\to 0} \frac{d}{dr} \log((x+1)^{r+1}-x^{r+1}) \\ &\quad = \lim_{r\to 0} \frac{(x+1)^{r+1}\log(x+1)-x^{r+1}\log x}{(x+1)^{r+1}-x^{r+1}} \\ &\quad = (x+1)\log(x+1)-x\log x, \end{align*} where $\log$ denotes natural logarithm. It follows that $g(x) = e^{(x+1)\log(x+1)-x\log x} = \frac{(x+1)^{x+1}}{x^x}$. Thus \[ \lim_{x\to\infty} \frac{g(x)}{d} = \left(\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{x+1}{d}\right) \cdot \left(\lim_{x\to\infty} \left(1+\frac{1}{d}\right)^x\right) = 1\cdot e = e. \]  The limit is $\boxed{e}$.

problem: Find the least positive integer $D$ such that a regular tetrahedron with vertices having integer coordinates can be inscribed within the sphere defined by the equation \[ a^2 + c^2 + e^2 = D. \]
solution: In one direction, for $D = 3m^2$, the points \[ (m,m,m), (m,-m,-m), (-m,m,-m), (-m,-m,m) \] form the vertices of a regular tetrahedron inscribed in the sphere $a^2 + c^2 + e^2 = D$.  Conversely, suppose that $P_i = (a_i, c_i, e_i)$ for $i=1,\dots,4$ are the vertices of an inscribed regular  tetrahedron. Then the center of this tetrahedron must equal the center of the sphere, namely $(0,0,0)$. Consequently, these four vertices together with $Q_i = (-a_i, -c_i, -e_i)$ for $i=1,\dots,4$ form the vertices of an inscribed cube in the sphere. The side length of this cube is $(D/3)^{1/2}$, so its volume is $(D/3)^{3/2}$; on the other hand, this volume also equals the determinant of the matrix with row vectors $Q_2-Q_1, Q_3-Q_1, Q_4-Q_1$, which is an integer. Hence $(D/3)^3$ is a perfect square, as then is $D/3$. The integers $D$ with this property are those of the form $3m^2$ for some positive integer $m$, making the smallest value $\boxed{3}$.

problem: Consider the set $R$ containing all integers $c$ between 1 and 2021 (inclusive) that are coprime to 2021. Define a function $J(r)$ for non-negative integers $r$ as follows: \[ J(r) = \sum_{c \in R} c^r. \] What is the largest value of $r$ below 2021 for which $J(r)$ is divisible by 2021?
solution:
We first check that for $p$ prime, \[ \sum_{c=1}^{p-1} c^r \equiv 0 \pmod{p} \Leftrightarrow j 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod{p-1}. \] If $r \equiv 0 \pmod{p-1}$, then $c^r \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ for each $c$, so $\sum_{c=1}^{p-1} c^r \equiv p-1 \pmod{p}$. If $r 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod{p-1}$, we can pick a primitive root $m$ modulo $p$, observe that $m^r 
ot\equiv 1 \pmod{p}$, and then note that \[ \sum_{c=1}^{p-1} c^r \equiv \sum_{c=1}^{p-1} (mc)^r = m^r \sum_{c=1}^{p-1} c^r \pmod{p}, \] which is only possible if $\sum_{c=1}^{p-1} c^r \equiv 0 \pmod{p}$.  We now note that the prime factorization of 2021 is $43 	imes 47$, so it suffices to determine when $J(r)$ is divisible by each of 43 and 47. We have  egin{align*} J(r) &\equiv 46 \sum_{c=1}^{42} c^r \pmod{43} \ J(r) &\equiv 42 \sum_{c=1}^{46} c^r \pmod{47}. \end{align*} Since 46 and 42 are coprime to 43 and 47, respectively,  we have   egin{gather*} J(r) \equiv 0 \pmod{43} \Leftrightarrow r 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod{42} \ J(r) \equiv 0 \pmod{47} \Leftrightarrow r 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod{46}. \end{gather*} This yields the claimed result. The values of $r$ in question are those not divisible by either $42$ or $46$, making the maximum value less than 2021,  oxed{2020}.

problem: Imagine a sequence of $3U$ real numbers. We can \emph{trim} this sequence to $U$ numbers using the following method: Partition the sequence into $U$ triplets, and for each triplet, retain only the middle value when ordered. Now, consider this process: Begin with $3^{52184}$ numbers, each chosen randomly and independently from a uniform distribution on [0,1]. Apply the trimming process described above, which results in $3^{52183}$ numbers. Continue trimming until a single number, $Q$, remains. If $\mu$ represents the expected value of $|Q - \frac{1}{2}|$, what is the smallest possible value of \mu?
solution: Let $f_k(x)$ be the probability distribution of $Q_k$, the last number remaining when one repeatedly trims a list of $3^k$ random variables chosen with respect to the uniform distribution on $[0,1]$; note that $f_0(x) = 1$ for $x \in [0,1]$. Let $F_k(x)=\int_0^x f_k(t)\,dt$ be the cumulative distribution function; by symmetry, $F_k(\frac{1}{2}) = \frac{1}{2}$. Let $\mu_k$ be the expected value of $Q_k - \frac{1}{2}$; then $\mu_0 = \frac{1}{4}$, so it will suffice to prove that $\mu_{k} \geq \frac{2}{3} \mu_{k-1}$ for $k > 0$.  By integration by parts and symmetry, we have \[ \mu_k = 2 \int_0^{1/2} \left( \frac{1}{2} - x \right) f_k(x)\,dx  = 2 \int_0^{1/2} F_k(x)\,dx; \] that is, $\mu_k$ computes twice the area under the curve $y = F_k(x)$ for $0 \leq x \leq\frac{1}{2}$. Since $F_k$ is a monotone function from $[0, \frac{1}{2}]$  with $F_k(0) = 0$ and $F_k(\frac{1}{2}) = \frac{1}{2}$, we may transpose the axes to obtain \begin{equation} \label{eq:2021B6 eq4} \mu_k = 2 \int_0^{1/2} \left( \frac{1}{2} - F_k^{-1}(y) \right)\,dy. \end{equation}  Since $f_k(x)$ is the probability distribution of the median of three random variables chosen with respect to the distribution $f_{k-1}(x)$, \begin{equation} \label{eq:2021B6 eq1} f_k(x) = 6 f_{k-1}(x) F_{k-1}(x) ( 1-F_{k-1}(x) ) \end{equation} or equivalently \begin{equation} \label{eq:2021B6 eq2} F_k(x) = 3 F_{k-1}(x)^2 - 2 F_{k-1}(x)^3. \end{equation} By induction, $F_k$ is the $k$-th iterate of $F_1(x) = 3x^2 -2x^3$, so \begin{equation} \label{eq:2021B6 eq5} F_k(x) = F_{k-1}(F_1(x)). \end{equation} Since $f_1(t) = 6t(1-t) \leq \frac{3}{2}$ for $t \in [0,\frac{1}{2}]$, \[ \frac{1}{2} - F_1(x) = \int_x^{1/2} 6t(1-t)\,dt \leq \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}-x\right); \] for $y \in [0, \frac{1}{2}]$, we may take $x = F_{k}^{-1}(y)$ to obtain \begin{equation} \label{eq:2021B6 eq3} \frac{1}{2} - F_k^{-1}(y) \geq \frac{2}{3} \left( \frac{1}{2} - F_{k-1}^{-1}(y) \right). \end{equation} Using \eqref{eq:2021B6 eq5} and \eqref{eq:2021B6 eq3}, we obtain \begin{align*} \mu_k &= 2 \int_0^{1/2} \left( \frac{1}{2} - F_k^{-1}(y) \right) \,dy \\ &\geq \frac{4}{3} \int_0^{1/2} \left( \frac{1}{2} - F_{k-1}^{-1}(y) \right) \,dy = \frac{2}{3}\mu_{k-1} \end{align*} as desired. The minimum value of \mu is \boxed{\frac{1}{4} \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^{52184}}.

The minimum value of \mu is \boxed{\frac{1}{4} \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^{16011}}.
